TextENGNew: "...So this is... all of it."
changeReason: Old phrasing is a question expressing uncertainty; new is a declarative statement with different framing ('I guess' vs 'So this is').

TextENGNew: Having finished reading through all the files... I let out a long, desolate sigh.
In the end, despite all the years of investigation and inquiry, I have uncovered nothing — not a single thing — about the mysteries concealed within the Great Hinamizawa Disaster.
And I am soon to step away from the very position from which those mysteries might be unraveled...
changeReason: New translation specifies stepping away from 'the position' rather than just 'investigating,' adding the nuance that his role itself was the means to solve the mysteries.

TextENGNew: (...How pitiful.)
Once I burned with conviction and swore to myself that I would see it through — and this is where I stand.
Such a thunderous beginning and such a feeble end. I can't even summon a mirthless laugh.
changeReason: Japanese 失笑 means an involuntary laugh/scoff; old uses 'scornful smile' while new uses 'mirthless laugh,' which is a different physical action and more accurate to the Japanese.

TextENGNew: "...I'm sorry, Rika-chan."
To the girl who is no longer of this world... I murmur those words of apology.
If none of it had happened back then, she would have grown, blossomed into a beautiful young woman...
And she should have been building a home, living a life full of happiness.
changeReason: Japanese 家庭を持って means 'starting/having her own household'; old translation implies living with existing family, while new correctly conveys building her own home/family.

TextENGNew: A radiant future, brimming with possibilities — that was what should have been hers.
That I could not protect it... no matter how many excuses I might heap up, the fault lies with me.
changeReason: Old says 'I wasn't able to help her'; new says 'the fault lies with me,' which shifts from inability to personal blame, closer to the Japanese 私だ (it's me/my fault).

TextENGNew: It was my powerlessness, my dullness of perception, that could not arrest the chain of misfortunes.
...Though, for all the self-reproach I might pour out here, nothing will change. It is all already over...
"...Ah, so you were here after all. I've been looking for you, Dad — no, Assistant Commissioner Akasaka."
A familiar voice, light as a bell rolling in darkness, reached me — and I turned toward it.
changeReason: Japanese 聞きなれた means 'familiar/accustomed to hearing'; old omits this, while new correctly includes 'familiar.'

TextENGNew: And standing there was my daughter... Miyuki.
"Come on, the handover business doesn't start until next week, right? You're as much of a workaholic as ever. The doctor is going to scold you again, you know?"
"Ah, yes... sorry."
I nearly apologized automatically — but then something caught my attention.
changeReason: Old says he 'immediately apologized'; new says he 'nearly apologized,' changing whether the apology was actually uttered. Japanese 謝りかけて means 'was about to apologize,' so new is more accurate.

TextENGNew: This was a confidential office within Public Security, wasn't it? With strict access controls?
"Speaking of which, Miyuki — how did you get in here? Outsiders should be off limits..."
"Yeah. I asked Kanou-san, and he let me in. Is there a problem?"
"...Considerably so."
Parent and child or not, regulations within a police organization are absolute. A violation calls for commensurate disciplinary action.
But senpai — why would you go out of your way to break the rules yourself?
"...Hmm, has he not reached out to you yet? Starting next month, I'm being transferred to Public Security. My posting will be here — the 7th Room, apparently."
"What...?"
As my daughter pointed her finger toward the floor, I heard a foolish noise come out of my own mouth.
...This was the first I'd heard of it. A personnel transfer this significant, and not a word to me...?
"So — since the handover period will be brief, I look forward to your guidance in the time we have. ...Please go easy on me."
Saying that, Miyuki gave an ostentatiously formal salute — then flashed me a brazen, satisfied smile.
And when I saw that... the person responsible for this came to me in a vague but unmistakable certainty.
changeReason: Japanese おぼろげに、しかし確実に means 'vaguely but certainly'; old says 'knew exactly,' losing the nuance of vagueness that the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: (...You got me.)
This had to be Kanou-senpai's parting gift — or rather, his final act of mischief.
changeReason: New translation includes 'parting gift' (手向け from the Japanese), which the old translation omits, changing the characterization of Kanou-senpai's action.

TextENGNew: The image of him grinning smugly to himself welled up and gave me an immediate headache.
changeReason: Japanese says 頭痛 (headache); old translates as 'nauseating' which is a different physical sensation. New correctly uses 'headache.'

TextENGNew: "What? What is it?"
"No... it's nothing."
Still — she used to be such a sweet girl... year by year she's grown more formidable. Brazen, almost.
How on earth did she end up with so many of these peculiarities?
"Well — given my father, I can hardly imagine. Don't ask me."
"...Hey, don't finish sentences I haven't said yet."
changeReason: Japanese explicitly says 'don't anticipate words I haven't spoken'; old loses the 'anticipating unspoken words' nuance, while new preserves it with 'don't finish sentences I haven't said yet.'

TextENGNew: "Well, your face says it all. ...Ehehe!"
"Now listen here. ...Ah..."
I tried to fire back — and then her smile, for just an instant,
changeReason: Old says 'a smiling face suddenly appeared before me' (implying a new face appearing); new says 'her smile, for just an instant' (referring to the daughter's existing smile). The Japanese refers to the daughter's smile, making the new more accurate.

TextENGNew: ...overlapped with someone else's face... and was gone.
...Once, long ago, something like this had happened before.
And in that moment, I had steeled my resolve and risen to unravel the mysteries of 'Hinamizawa'...!
Yes, that's right. It is not over yet.
As long as I do not give up... as long as my will does not die, the possibility of uncovering the truth will never reach zero!
And even as I leave this post midway through — my daughter, Miyuki, is stepping into this very place to carry on!
"...I'm sorry, Miyuki."
"Huh?"
"There is something I need to discuss with you. Something important — something that perhaps only you can help me with. ...Will you hear me out?"
changeReason: Japanese 相談 means 'consult/discuss'; old uses 'tell you' (one-directional), new uses 'discuss with you' (collaborative), which changes the nature of the request.

TextENGNew: I want to believe in fate. ...No — I will believe in it.
With that vow in my heart, I turned to face her.
